verses.commentary: Geography matters. Kidron: east of Jerusalem. David crossed fleeing Absalom (2 Samuel 15:23). Garden: Gethsemane. Parallels begin.
Regular meeting spot. Judas exploits intimacy. Betrayal enabled by knowledge.
Speira (σπεῖρα), cohort, 600 soldiers. Excessive force for one man. Temple police included. Torches suggest night. Weapons for violent arrest.
Jesus, knowing all that was going to happen to Him, went out and asked them, Who is it you want? Divine foreknowledge. No surprise. Jesus initiates. Meets them. Bold question.
Jesus of Nazareth. I am He. And Judas the traitor was standing there with them. They identify target. Egō eimi (ἐγώ εἰμι), I AM. Divine name. Judas present, noted for emphasis.
When Jesus said, I am He, they drew back and fell to the ground. Divine power. Egō eimi causes collapse. Backward fall. Involuntary prostration. Jesus' authority displayed even in arrest.
Again He asked them, Who is it you want? Repetition. Second chance. Persistent question.
Jesus answered, I told you that I am He. If you are looking for Me, then let these men go. Self-identification repeated. Protection for disciples. Shepherd guards sheep.
This happened so that the words He had spoken would be fulfilled: I have not lost one of those You gave Me. John 17:12 fulfilled. Protection promise kept. Sovereign care.

Then Simon Peter, who had a sword, drew it and struck the high priest's servant, cutting off his right ear. The servant's name was Malchus. Peter's violence. Sword drawn. Ear severed. Right ear specified. Malchus named. Impulsive defense.
Jesus commanded Peter, Put your sword away! Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given Me? Rebuke. Cup: suffering, death. Divine will. No resistance. Acceptance of Father's plan.
Arrest completed. Edēsan (ἔδησαν), bound. King in chains. Irony.
Former high priest. Power behind throne. Caiaphas identified.
Ironic prophecy. One dies for many.
Another disciple (John). Access through connection. Courtyard entry.
John returns. Speaks to doorkeeper. Gains Peter's entrance.
You aren't one of this man's disciples too, are you? She asked Peter. He replied, I am not. First denial. Servant girl's question. Easy denial. Cowardice begins.
Fire for warmth. Peter with enemies. Warming with arresters. Dangerous association.
Disciples. Teaching. Seeking grounds for charges.

I have spoken openly to the world. I always taught in synagogues or at the temple, where all the Jews come together. I said nothing in secret. Defense. Public ministry. Open teaching. No secret doctrine. Nothing hidden.
Why question Me? Ask those who heard Me. Surely they know what I said. Appeal to witnesses. Legal principle. Hearers can testify. Transfer burden of proof.
When Jesus said this, one of the officials nearby slapped Him in the face. Is this the way You answer the high priest? Violence. Slap for perceived disrespect. Authoritarian reaction.

If I said something wrong, testify as to what is wrong. But if I spoke the truth, why did You strike Me? Challenge. Demand for evidence. If wrong, prove it. If right, why violence? Logic confronts brutality.
Still bound. Official trial venue.

Meanwhile, Simon Peter was still standing there warming himself. So they asked him, You aren't one of His disciples too, are you? He denied it, saying, I am not. Second denial. Same fire. Different questioners. Same answer. Ouk eimi (οὐκ εἰμί), I am not. Opposite of Jesus' Egō eimi.

One of the high priest's servants, a relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off, challenged him, Didn't I see you with Him in the garden? Third questioner. Relative of Malchus. Eyewitness. Direct accusation.
Immediate fulfillment. Rooster crows. Jesus' prophecy validated. Peter's boast shattered.
Dawn. Ironic purity concern. Avoid defilement by Gentile building while condemning innocent man. Strain gnats, swallow camels.
So Pilate came out to them and asked, What charges are you bringing against this man? Pilate accommodates. Goes outside. Requests formal charge.
If He were not a criminal, we would not have handed Him over to you. Evasive answer. No specific charge. Assume criminality. Demand trust without evidence.

Pilate said, Take Him yourselves and judge Him by your own law. But we have no right to execute anyone. Pilate deflects. Use your law. They reveal motive. Want Roman execution. Death penalty required.
Roman method. John 12:32-33. Lifted up. Prophecy fulfilled.
Pilate then went back inside the palace, summoned Jesus and asked Him, Are you the king of the Jews? Returns inside. Private interrogation. Political question. Kingship investigated.
Is that your own idea, or did others talk to you about Me? Jesus' counter-question. Source of charge? Pilate's idea or accusers'? Clarification sought.
Am I a Jew? Your own people and chief priests handed You over to me. What is it You have done? Pilate's frustration. Not my jurisdiction. Your people accuse. What did You do?

My kingdom is not of this world. If it were, My servants would fight to prevent My arrest by the Jewish leaders. But now My kingdom is from another place. Kingdom defined. Not earthly. No armed resistance. Non-worldly origin. Political threat dissolved.

You are a king, then! Jesus answered, You say that I am a king. In fact, the reason I was born and came into the world is to testify to the truth. Everyone on the side of truth listens to Me. Affirmation. King of truth. Mission: bear witness to truth. Truth-seekers hear Him.

What is truth? With this he went out again to the Jews gathered there and said, I find no basis for a charge against Him. Pilate's cynical question. Doesn't wait for answer. Returns outside. Verdict: Not guilty. First acquittal.

But it is your custom for me to release to you one prisoner at the time of the Passover. Do you want me to release the king of the Jews? Passover amnesty. Offer to release. Calls Him king. Provocative title. Expects yes.
They shouted back, No, not Him! Give us Barabbas! Rejection. Barabbas chosen. Lēstēs (λῃστής), robber, revolutionary. They prefer criminal to Messiah. Murderer over Savior.
